pages.text: Evaluation of the induction healing effect of porous asphalt concrete through four point bending fatigue test Quantao Liu a,⇑, Erik Schlangen a, Martin van de Ven b, Gerbert van Bochove c, Jo van Montfort d a Delft University of Technology, Faculty of Civil Engineering and Geosciences, Micromechanics Laboratory (MICROLAB), Stevinweg 1, 2628 CN Delft, T he Netherlands b Delft University of Technology, Faculty of Civil Engineering and Geosciences, Road and Railway Engineering, Stevinweg 1, 2628 CN Delft, The Netherl ands c Heijmans-Breijn, Graafsebaan 65, 5248 JT Rosmalen, The Netherlands d SGS-Intron, Sittard, Dr Nolenslaan 126, 6136 GV Sittard, The Netherlands article info Article history: Received 8 July 2011 Received in revised form 19 September 2011 Accepted 2 October 2011 Available online 29 November 2011 Keywords: Porous asphalt concrete Steel wool Induction heating Healing Four point bending test abstract The objective of this paper is to evaluate the induction healing effect of steel wool reinforced porous asphalt concrete. The four point bending fatigue resistance of the beams was ﬁrst studied. It was found that the fatigue resistance of these steel wool reinforced beams was quite good compared with the ref- erences in the literature. Then, fatigue life extension ratio and ﬂexural stiffness recovery of the beams were used to show their healing effect. The fatigue life extension ratio was measured after introducing induction heating and letting the fatigue damaged beams rest. It was found that induction heating increases the healing rate of the beams and that the healing is highly microstrain-dependent with higher healing rate under high microstrain level. It was also found that the optimal heating temperature is 85 /C176C to obtain the best healing effect. Heating too much can cause swelling in the specimens, which will decrease the total healing produced. The fatigue damaged beams obtained extra stiffness recovery when induction heating was applied to them. Finally, it was also found that fatigue life of porous asphalt con- crete can be signiﬁcantly extended by applying multiple induction heating. Based on these ﬁndings, it is concluded that the self healing effect of porous asphalt concrete can be increased by induction heating the material. Additionally, the durability of porous asphalt pavement will also be improved with induc- tion healing. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Netherlands is a densely populated country and many people live close to the motorways, so trafﬁc noise is one of the most seri- ous environmental issues. To reduce trafﬁc noise, porous asphalt was ﬁrst utilized as a surface wearing course in 1972 and it was decided to start applying porous asphalt surface wearing courses on a larger scale in 1987 [1,2]. Up to now, close to 90% of the Dutch motorway networks is surfaced with porous asphalt concrete and it is a government policy to apply porous asphalt on the whole motorway network [3]. Standard porous asphalt PA 0/16 is used mostly as a surface wearing course with a thickness of 50 mm and a minimum air voids content of 20%. This open structure can reduce trafﬁc noise by 3 db [4,5]. Besides, porous asphalt also re- duces spray and splash and prevents aquaplaning under wet con- ditions, which can improve the driving comfort and safety [6,7]. However, porous asphalt does not last long. Compared with the lifetime of dense graded asphalt concrete roads, which can survive up to 18 ye

samples could recover more than 50% of its fatigue resistance when the samples were let to rest for one day and a 1.47 kPa pres- sure was used to press the crack faces together during this rest per- iod. Some laboratory experiments done by Van Dijk and Francken demonstrated that the fatigue of asphalt mixture could be ex- tended when rest periods were introduced in between the contin- uous loading regimes [17,18]. Both the recovery of strength and the extension of fatigue life were the evidence of healing induced by rest periods. Healing of asphalt concrete can also be found in the ﬁeld [19,20]. Williams et al. used surface wave measurement to assess the stiffness of pavement before, immediately after, and 24 h after loading passes respectively. Stiffness was reported to re- cover after 24 h rest [19]. The problem is that the self healing recovery of asphalt pave- ment is very slow at ambient temperature, and the trafﬁc circula- tion on a road cannot be blocked to allow enough self healing recovery. However, it is feasible to enhance the self healing capac- ity and speed of asphalt concrete by increasing its temperature. The self healing of asphalt concrete is a temperature dependent behavior and the amount of healing increases when the material is subjected to a higher temperature during the rest period [21– 25]. An increase in the test temperature also reduces the total recovery time [25]. As asphalt concrete has already shown better self healing rates at higher temperatures during rest periods [21– 25], the induction healing approach is very promising. As shown in Fig. 1 , induction healing consists in adding steel wool to porous asphalt concrete to make it electrically conductive and suitable for induction heating [26,27]. When small cracks oc- cur in the pavement, the temperature of the mastic can be in- creased locally by induction heating to make the bitumen soft enough to start its self healing process so that porous asphalt con- crete can repair itself and ravelling can be delayed [27,28]. This developed porous asphalt has two beneﬁts compared with normal porous asphalt. The ﬁrst one is the steel wool reinforcement effect and the other one is to enhance the healing rate and speed of por- ous asphalt concrete via induction heating [27–29] . In the previous research, healing was evaluated in indirect tensile fatigue test, but during the test permanent deformation occurred. To avoid the per- manent deformation healing was evaluated via four point bending fatigue test in this research. The objective of this research is to evaluate the induction heal- ing effect of porous asphalt concrete through four point bending test. Another purpose of this research is to determine the optimal heating temperature and the starting point of induction heating. The possibility of multiple heating is also investigated. 2. Materials The dimensions of the porous asphalt concrete beams used in this research were 50 mm /C2 50 mm /C2 400 mm. These beams were prepared with the same mate- rials as used in a test section on Dutch motorway A58, that is specially prepared to investigate the induction healing approach. This mixture consisted of standard porous asphalt PA 0/16 and 1.27% steel wool (mass content of the total mixture). The steel wool had a length of 10 mm and diameters ranging from 8.89 lmt o 12.79 lm. It was difﬁcult to disperse the steel wool into the mixture. Adding more steel wool than 1.27% to the mixture caused mixing problem. 3. Experimenta

of the induction heating machine and the top surface of the sample was varied from 30 mm to 20 mm and 10 mm to study its effect on the heating speed of the sample. The beams were heated for 3 min and the temperature changes of the beams were measured with a 320 /C2 240 pixels, full color infrared camera. To get a better under- standing of the induction heating system, induction heating image of a porous as- phalt concrete beam is shown in Fig. 3 . As the main goal of this induction healing approach is to prevent the surface damage ravelling, the surface temperature of the pavement after heating is most important. So, these experiments focus on the temperature increase speed at the surface of the beams during heating. 3.3. Fatigue life extension test First, to compare natural healing and induction healing and to analyze the inﬂu- ence of strain amplitude on the healing rate, the fatigue life extension of the beams caused by natural rest periods and induction heating were studied under four dif- ferent strain amplitudes (200–250–300–400 microstrain), at a frequency of 8 Hz. The test procedure consisted in damaging the beam in four-point bending fatigue test until its ﬂexural stiffness was reduced to 50% of the initial value, then rest the beam for 18 h directly or heat the beam to 70 /C176C with the induction heating ma- chine and then rest it for 18 h, and ﬁnally damage the beam again with fatigue load- ing. The second time fatigue life can be seen as a healing indication caused by resting and induction heating. As shown in Fig. 4 , the healing index fatigue life extension ratio can be deﬁned as the extra fatigue life ( Df) after damaging divided by its original fatigue life ( f). This relative healing indication eliminates the inﬂu- ence of the absolute fatigue life on the healing rate. Second, to qualify the effect of temperature on the healing rate and to deter- mine the optimal heating temperature, the extra fatigue life of the damaged beams with different heating and resting temperatures were investigated. For that, the beams were damaged with a constant fatigue loading stain amplitude of 300 micro- strain (8 Hz). Then, the beams were induction heated to three different tempera- tures 70 /C176C, 85 /C176C, 100 /C176C respectively and rested at 20 /C176C for 18 h, or directly rested at 20 /C176Co r5 /C176C for 18 h. Finally the fatigue lives of beams were measured again with the same stain amplitude. Third, the relationship between the damage extent and the healing rate was studied to get an idea of the optimal time to start induction heating. As shown in Fig. 5 , the beams were loaded 50,000, 60,000, 70,000 cycles or loaded until failure, respectively, with 300 microstrain at a frequency of 8 Hz. The damage extent can be described as the loading cycles divided by the original fatigue life (95,700 deter- mined from the fatigue line of this mixture). So 50,000, 60,000, 70,000 cycles’ load- ing corresponds to a damage extent of 52.2%, 62.7%, 73.1% respectively. Then the damaged beams were heated to 85 /C176C and rested for 18 h. Finally, the fatigue lives of healed beams were measured with the same stain amplitude of 300 microstrain. 3.4. Flexural stiffness recovery measurement The ﬂexural stiffness recovery of a damaged beam was measured in this re- search to characterize how the healing recovery developed with the resting time and to compare the differences between natural healing and induction healing. Firstly, the ﬂexural stiffness

However, when the sample was heated to 85 /C176C with induction heating machine and rested for 3 h, the stiffness was found to re- cover to 2522 MPa, very close to the original stiffness value. This jump of stiffness (extra stiffness recovery) was a proof of healing which was caused by induction heating. So, it can be concluded that induction healing helps to restore the stiffness of steel wool reinforced porous asphalt concrete. Compared with the fatigue life extension ratio of 63.9% under the same damage extent in Fig. 10 , the stiffness is more recovered. It means that the stiffness is easier to recover than the fatigue life. 4.5. Application of multiple times induction heating on porous asphalt concrete beams The original fatigue curve and the multiple times induction heating modiﬁed fatigue curve of the samples are compared in Fig. 12 . The original fatigue life of the sample is 95, 700 cycles. With 4 times damage loading of 50,000 cycles followed by 4 times induction heating and resting, the modiﬁed fatigue life is 277, 720, which is 2.9 times the original fatigue life. The fatigue life exten- sion ratio is 190%. It is found that multiple times induction heating can greatly extent the fatigue life of porous asphalt concrete, although the moment of induction heating of the pavement and the frequency are not yet fully optimized. 5. Conclusions The fatigue resistance and the healing effect of steel wool rein- forced porous asphalt concrete beams were studied in this re- search. Fatigue life extension ratio and stiffness recovery of the beams were used as healing indications. Based on the test results, the following conclusions can be drawn: 1. The fatigue resistance of these beams is quite good com- pared with the references in the literatures in this research. 2. Induction heating increases the fatigue life extension ratio (healing rate) of the fatigue damaged beams and the heal- ing rate is highly microstrain dependent with higher heal- ing rate under higher microstrain level. 3. The optimal heating temperature is 85 /C176C to obtain the best healing effect. Heating too much causes swelling and bin- der drainage problems, which will decrease the healing. 4. The stiffness of the fatigued beams recovers more and much faster when induction heating is applied to them. 5. The fatigue life of porous asphalt concrete can be signiﬁ- cantly extended by multiple times induction heating. 6. It is expected that the self healing effect and the durability of porous asphalt pavement will be improved by induction healing. Rotating surface abrasion test (RSAT) will be done in the future to determine the optimal starting point of induction heating on pavement, because this test is highly relevant to the pavement per- formance (24 h rotation on the sample is used to simulate 7 years trafﬁc circulation on ﬁeld). Field inspection of the situation of the road is also necessary to decide on the implementation of induc- tion heating. Ministry of transportation, public works and water manag

phalt concrete beam for 50,000 cycles. Then, the sample was induction heated to 85 /C176C and rested for 18 h for the ﬁrst time. After that, another 50,000 cycles fatigue loading was applied on the beams, followed by the second time heating and resting process. The damaging, heating/resting and re-damaging process was repeated four times. Finally, the fatigue life of the beam was measured. 4. Results and discussions 4.1. Fatigue resistance of steel wool reinforced porous asphalt concrete beams The fatigue line of the porous asphalt concrete beams is shown in Fig. 6, where e6 equals to 165 microstrain. To evaluate the fatigue resistance of this mixture, e6 of dense graded asphalt mixtures are used as references for lacking control beams and lacking of such references on porous asphalt concrete. Pang conducted four point bending fatigue test on three dense graded asphalt mixtures at 20 /C176C/10 Hz and obtained e6 ranging from 158, 168 to 184 micro- strains [30]. Compared with the results from Pang’s work, the fati- gue resistance of this porous asphalt concrete is quite good, comparable to that of dense graded asphalt concrete. 4.2. Induction heating speed of steel wool reinforced porous asphalt concrete beams With the software of the infrared camera, the temperature in- crease at the top surface of the sample can be monitored. Fig. 7 shows the relationship between the increase of the average tem- perature at the top surface of the beam and the heating time. It can be seen that the temperature increases somewhat faster in the ﬁrst 15 s and after that the temperature increases almost line- arly. The mean heating speed at the top surface of the beam is cal- culated as the temperature increase during the heating process divided by the heating time. It is also found that the beams can be heated much faster when they are closer to the coil of the induc- tion heating machine. The mean heating speed of the beams with different distances between the sample and the coil are summa- rized in Table 1 . To obtain a higher induction speed, the distance will be ﬁxed at 10 mm when heating the samples in the subse- quent healing tests, where an induction heating speed of 0.3407 /C176C/s can be obtained. Given this heating speed, the heating time needed for the sample to reach a certain temperature can be calculated. For instance, to increase the surface temperature from 20 /C176Ct o8 5 /C176C, the beams should be heated for 190 s. Although this heating time is unpractical for real road application, it has no inﬂu- ence on the objective of this research: to prove that induction heat- ing can increase the healing rate of porous asphalt concrete. Now, we are trying to optimize the induction generator to enhance its heating efﬁciency. 4.3. Fatigue life extension ratio of steel wool reinforced porous asphalt concrete beams caused by induction heating The fatigue life extension ratios of porous asphalt concrete beams with natural healing and induction healing are plotted in Fig. 8. It is found that under all 4 microstrain amplitudes induction heating increases the fatigue life extension ratios. However, these healing increments are not very high. The ﬁrst reason for this is that the temperature increment after induction heating is very lim- ited, because the temperature decreases to the rest temperature of 20 /C176C very quickly in 2 h. The temperature difference is only in the ﬁrst 2 h, so the healing difference in these two cases is limited. It is expected t
